title: Cell communication
description: Test your knowledge of cell communication!

assessment_items.question_content:
During the cell-mediated immune response, killer T-cells target and kill infected cells. This process is shown in the image below. 
[[☃ image 1]]

*Image credit: Adapted from Dananguyen [CC BY-SA 4.0], via Wikimedia Commons*

During an experiment, researchers introduced a substance that caused the Class I MHC molecule to break down.

**Which of the following best describes how the cell-mediated immune response will be affected?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A diagram showing the interaction of an infected cell with a killer T cell. The first image shows an infected cell that has a class I MHC molecule attached to it, and the class I MHC is attached at the other end to a killer T cell. Inside the killer T cell there are multiple colored dots labeled local regulators. There is an arrow from the first image pointing at the second image which shows the local regulators being released from the killer T cell. There is an arrow pointing the surface of the infected cell, and there are local regulators inside the infected cell. From the second image there are 2 arrows. One arrow shows the killer T cell separated from the infected cell and it is labeled released killer T cell. The second arrow points from the infected cell towards a cell that is starting to fragment and break apart and it is labeled dying infected cell.

choices.content: The killer T-cell will no longer bind to the infected cell, and as a result, the infected cell will not be killed.
The killer T-cell will still bind to the infected cell, but the infected cell will not be killed.
The killer T-cell will no longer bind to the infected cell, and the infected cell will instead be killed by killer T-cells releasing long-distance signals.
The killer T-cell will still bind to the infected cell, but the infected cell will instead be killed by a helper T-cell.

assessment_items.question_content:
During the humoral immune response, helper T-cells activate B-cells through cell communication. This process is shown in the image below. 
[[☃ image 1]]

*Image credit: Adapted from OpenStax College [CC BY 3.0], via Wikimedia Commons*

During an experiment, researchers introduced a substance that caused the T-cell receptor to break down.

**Which of the following best describes how the humoral immune response will be affected?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A B cell is shown with a Y shaped B cell receptor that has an antigen attached to one arm of the Y. There is an arrow pointing from the B cell towards the same image of the B cell, but with an additional green receptor on the surface and small red dots inside the B cell. Attached to the additional green receptor is a helper T cell, with an arrow pointing to a T cell receptor that is connected to the B cell receptor. The B cell is labeled activated B cell. The plasma membrane of the T cell shows many red dots being released from the T cell and the dots are labeled local regulator.

choices.content: The helper T-cell will no longer bind to the B-cell, and as a result, the B-cell will not be activated.
The helper T-cell will still bind to the B-cell, but the B-cell will not be activated.
The helper T-cell will no longer bind to the B-cell, and the B-cell will instead be activated by helper T-cells releasing long-distance signals.
The helper T-cell will still bind to the B-cell, but the B-cell will instead be activated by a killer T-cell.

assessment_items.question_content:
During cell communication in the nervous system, chemical messengers called neurotransmitters travel between neurons. As they travel, neurotransmitters send messages from one neuron to another.  

**Which of the following best describes the role of neurotransmitters in neuron-to-neuron communication?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Neurotransmitters are local regulators that allow neurons to communicate over short distances.
Neurotransmitters are local regulators that allow neurons to communicate over long distances.
Neurotransmitters are long-distance signaling molecules that allow neurons to communicate over long distances.
Neurotransmitters are long-distance signaling molecules that allow neurons to communicate over short distances.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

*Image credit: Adapted from CNX OpenStax [CC BY 4.0], via Wikimedia Commons*

**Which of the following best describes the cell communication occurring in the figure above?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A presynaptic cell is shown with 3 vesicles and inside the vesicles are 3 small balls labeled neurotransmitter. Two of the vesicles are shown at the end of the presynaptic cell releasing neurotransmitters into a space labeled synapse. On the other side of the space the postsynaptic cell is labeled, and there are protrusions on the postsynaptic cell labeled that the neurotransmitter is sitting on. The structure is labeled neurotransmitter attached to receptor.

choices.content: A signal-emitting cell communicates with a nearby cell by releasing local regulators.
A signal-emitting cell communicates with a far-away cell by releasing long-distance signaling molecules.
A signal-emitting cell communicates with a nearby cell through direct cell-to-cell contact.
A signal-emitting cell communicates with a far-away cell by releasing local regulators.

assessment_items.question_content:
To complete external fertilization in fish, sperm cells must swim through open water in order to fertilize egg cells. During this process, females release a substance called ovarian fluid. Researchers hypothesized that ovarian fluid may play a role in attracting sperm cells towards egg cells. 

To test this hypothesis, researchers performed an experiment in which they observed the swimming paths of sperm cells as they moved towards egg cells. The researchers recorded swimming paths in water (A) and ovarian fluid (B), as shown in the image below. 


[[☃ image 1]]

Sperm cell swimming paths in water (A) and ovarian fluid (B). Scale bars represent $25\, \mu\text{m}$.

*Yeates, S. E., Diamond, S. E., Einum, S. , Emerson, B. C., Holt, W. V. and Gage, M. J. (2013), Cryptic choice of conspecific sperm controlled by the impact of ovarian fluid on sperm swimming behavior. Evolution, 67: 3523-3536. doi:10.1111/evo.12208*

**Which of the following conclusions about ovarian fluid is best supported by the information above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The sketch in figure A shows multiple circular lines. The sketch in figure B shows multiple straight lines. Each sketch has a short horizontal line in the top right corner. There is no distinct pattern of organization in either sketch.

choices.content: Ovarian fluid contains long-distance signaling molecules that help sperm cells swim straighter, making them more likely to fertilize eggs.
Ovarian fluid contains long-distance signaling molecules that help sperm cells swim straighter, making them less likely to fertilize eggs.
Ovarian fluid contains local regulators that help sperm cells swim straighter, making them more likely to fertilize eggs.
Ovarian fluid contains local regulators that help sperm cells swim straighter, making them less likely to fertilize eggs.

assessment_items.question_content:
To complete external fertilization in fish, sperm cells must swim through open water in order to fertilize egg cells. During this process, females release a substance called ovarian fluid, which helps guide the sperm cells toward the egg cells. Researchers hypothesized that sperm cells are mainly attracted by ovarian fluid released from the same species.

To test this hypothesis, researchers performed an experiment in which they studied the effects of salmon ovarian fluid and trout ovarian fluid on salmon sperm. In each trial within the experiment, the researchers placed salmon sperm on one side of a porous membrane and either salmon ovarian fluid, trout ovarian fluid, or water on the other side. After two minutes, the researchers counted how many salmon sperm had passed through the membrane and into the fluid. The results are presented in the graph below.



[[☃ image 1]]

Mean numbers $\pm$SE of salmon sperm passing through porous membrane into salmon ovarian fluid (black bar), trout ovarian fluid (grey bar), and water (white bar).

*Yeates, S. E., Diamond, S. E., Einum, S. , Emerson, B. C., Holt, W. V. and Gage, M. J. (2013), Cryptic choice of conspecific sperm controlled by the impact of ovarian fluid on sperm swimming behavior. Evolution, 67: 3523-3536. doi:10.1111/evo.12208*

**Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the data above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Two Bar graphs are shown: one for for Salmon sperm and the other for trout sperm. The y axis is labeled sperm number, times 1000. The increments are the y axis are 0, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500. There are 3 bars for the salmon sperm, labeled salmon ovarian fluid, trout ovarian fluid and water. The y-axis value for the salmon ovarian fluid is approximately 1700 with an error bar up to approximately 2200. The y -axis value for the trout ovarian fluid is approximately 600 with an error bar extending from approximately 450 to 750. The y-axis value for water is approximately 550 with an error bar extending from approximately 450 to 550. There are 3 bars for trout sperm, labeled trout ovarian fluid, salmon ovarian fluid and water. The y-axis value for the trout ovarian fluid is approximately 1100 with an error bar extending up to approximately 1500. The y axis value for salmon ovarian fluid is approximately 400 with an error bar extending from approximately 300 to 600. The y axis value for water is approximately 300 with an error bar extending from approximately 250 to 350. The lower edge of the error bar on the salmon ovarian fluid and the trout ovarian fluid can not be seen because of the shading of the bars.

choices.content: Salmon sperm respond more strongly to long-distance signaling molecules in salmon ovarian fluid than to those in trout ovarian fluid.
Salmon sperm respond more strongly to local regulators in salmon ovarian fluid than to those in trout ovarian fluid.
Salmon sperm respond more strongly to long-distance signaling molecules in trout ovarian fluid than to those in salmon ovarian fluid.
Salmon sperm respond more strongly to local regulators in trout ovarian fluid than to those in salmon ovarian fluid.

assessment_items.question_content:
The tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) is a plant virus that infects a wide range of plants, including tobacco. In order to enter and travel through the cells of a plant, TMV produces a movement protein. This protein helps the virus travel directly between the cytoplasms of interconnected plant cells.

**Which of the following best predicts how the movement protein modifies plant cells in order to help TMV travel from cell-to-cell?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The movement protein modifies the plasmodesmata between plant cells.
The movement protein modifies the vacuoles within plant cells.
The movement protein increases the formation of transport vesicles within plant cells.
The movement protein increases the formation of cell walls between plant cells.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following is an excerpt from a scientific paper written by Levy et al. 

>The plant cell wall is a barrier to virus exit from and entry into cells. Thus, successful infection requires that plant viruses encode movement proteins to transport the viral genome locally cell-to-cell within a leaf and on into the vascular system, through which the virus will systematically invade the plant.

>*Levy, A., Zheng, J. Y., & Lazarowitz, S. G. (2013). The Tobamovirus Turnip Vein Clearing Virus 30-Kilodalton Movement Protein Localizes to Novel Nuclear Filaments To Enhance Virus Infection. Journal of Virology, 87(11), 6428–6440. doi:10.1128/jvi.03390-12*

**Which of the following best predicts how movement proteins help plant viruses travel from one plant cell to another?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Movement proteins increase the permeability of the plasmodesmata between plant cells.
Movement proteins increase the movement of vacuoles between plant cells.
Movement proteins increase the movement of transport vesicles between plant cells.
Movement proteins increase the surface area of plasma membranes between plant cells.
